text: This sounds a bit like <https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy|scrcpy> for Android. It can either use the Android API for USB debugging, or what it calls <https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/blob/master/doc/otg.md|OTG mode>, which is similar to what you describe.

text: yep, it's like scrcpy for app interop

text: That would make a nice add-on functionality for virtualization tools such as QEMU.

text: Just saw this myself...! I am tempted to try building a complex system in this style to try it out. I do like that this approach keeps its diagrams up to date by generating them from actual program executions.

elements.text: Just saw this myself...! I am tempted to try building a complex system in this style to try it out. I do like that this approach keeps its diagrams up to date by generating them from actual program executions.

text:
It does remind me of this snippet from the Inform 7 literate programming docs (<https://ganelson.github.io/inweb/inweb/M-iti.html>) though:

> ... LP is not much practiced, but it is also because, as Christopher Wyk perceptively observed (CACM 33.3, 1990), "no one has yet volunteered to write a program using another's system for literate programming".

elements.text: It does remind me of this snippet from the Inform 7 literate programming docs (

... LP is not much practiced, but it is also because, as Christopher Wyk perceptively observed (CACM 33.3, 1990), "no one has yet volunteered to write a program using another's system for literate programming".

text: Hopefully one of these literate systems may eventually escape and see use beyond the original creator. It does seem like this kind of literate documentation would aid with Naur-style "theory building" (communicating the head space of a program) for others that come along later.
